metadata.subject: Physical and Health Education
metadata.description: Fitness and Conditioning 12 advances student understanding of sports science, periodization, and performance optimization. Students explore advanced training concepts including macrocyclic planning, sport-specific conditioning, and the integration of science-based recovery and nutrition strategies. The course prepares students for fitness leadership roles through advanced program design, understanding of rehabilitation and injury prevention, and ability to guide others' conditioning development. Students examine performance data analytically, understand training adaptations at a physiological level, and consider career pathways in fitness science, coaching, athletic training, and sports medicine.
metadata.general_description: An advanced course in training science and performance development, preparing students for fitness leadership, athletic coaching, and careers in sports science and health.

big_ideas.statement: Advanced periodization and strategic planning maximize athletic performance and fitness development across competitive and non-competitive contexts.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 12 students learn to design comprehensive multi-year plans with carefully sequenced training phases, managing fatigue, adaptation, and peak performance timing.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding physiological adaptations to training — at cellular and systemic levels — informs evidence-based program design and performance optimization.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students learn the science of how the body responds and adapts to different training stimuli, enabling intelligent program modifications and performance prediction.

big_ideas.statement: Sport-specific conditioning tailored to the demands of particular sports or activities optimizes performance and reduces injury risk.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students learn to analyze sport demands, identify required fitness components, and design training specifically targeting performance requirements.

big_ideas.statement: Recovery, rehabilitation, and injury prevention are sophisticated sciences integral to sustainable high-performance training.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students understand mechanisms of injury, recovery physiology, and evidence-based strategies for preventing injuries and returning safely to activity.

big_ideas.statement: Fitness leadership means guiding athletes and fitness enthusiasts toward sustainable excellence through science-based knowledge and mentorship.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop the ability to assess athlete needs, design comprehensive programs, monitor progress, and mentor athletes toward their potential.

categories.name: Advanced Periodization and Training Planning
categories.raw: Designing comprehensive multi-year training plans with strategic periodization.

competencies.raw: Design macrocycles, mesocycles, and microcycles; integrate multiple training qualities; manage training load and fatigue; apply periodization to competitive sport and fitness development; adapt plans based on performance data.
i_can_statements: I can design a multi-year macrocyclic training plan that strategically phases development, peaking, and recovery across seasons or competition schedules.
I can structure mesocycles (4-6 week blocks) with progressive overload, adaptation phases, and recovery emphasis aligned with overall goals.
I can integrate multiple fitness qualities — strength, power, endurance, agility, technique — within a coherent periodic structure.
I can manage cumulative training load, monitor fatigue and readiness, and adjust plans to prevent overtraining and optimize adaptation.
I can analyze performance data and athlete feedback to modify plans in response to actual adaptation and progress.

categories.name: Sport Science and Performance Physiology
categories.raw: Understanding physiological adaptations to training and applying sports science principles.

competencies.raw: Understand physiological training adaptations; apply sports science to program design; analyze training demands; predict performance outcomes; use data to guide training modifications.
i_can_statements: I can explain how different training stimuli trigger physiological adaptations — including muscle hypertrophy, aerobic capacity, neural efficiency, and metabolic changes.
I can analyze the specific physiological demands of different sports — energy systems, muscle groups, movement patterns, and conditioning requirements.
I can design training that specifically targets the physiological demands of a sport, progressively building required capacities.
I can interpret performance data — lactate thresholds, VO2 measurements, power outputs, biomechanics — to assess training effects and guide program decisions.
I can predict likely performance outcomes based on training progression, fitness levels, and historical data patterns.

categories.name: Sport-Specific and Advanced Conditioning
categories.raw: Applying conditioning principles to specific sports and performance contexts.

competencies.raw: Analyze sport-specific demands; design sport-specific training programs; integrate technique and conditioning; train energy systems appropriately; apply sport-specific testing and assessment.
i_can_statements: I can comprehensively analyze the physiological, biomechanical, and cognitive demands of a specific sport or athletic context.
I can design training programs that directly develop the capacities most important for performance in a specific sport.
I can integrate technical skill development with physical conditioning, ensuring both develop appropriately together.
I can select and apply appropriate training methods for the energy systems critical to performance — aerobic, anaerobic alactic, anaerobic lactic.
I can use sport-specific performance tests and metrics to assess fitness development and guide training modifications.

categories.name: Injury Prevention and Rehabilitation
categories.raw: Understanding injury mechanisms and applying evidence-based prevention and rehabilitation strategies.

competencies.raw: Understand common injury mechanisms; apply injury prevention strategies; recognize contraindications; guide return-to-play decisions; understand rehabilitation principles; support athletes through injury.
i_can_statements: I can identify movement patterns and training practices that increase injury risk, and design modifications to reduce injury probability.
I can recognize muscle imbalances, flexibility limitations, or movement dysfunctions that predispose to injury.
I can implement injury prevention training — including proprioceptive training, dynamic stability work, and movement quality focus.
I can understand basic rehabilitation principles and work collaboratively with healthcare providers in guiding return-to-sport progressions.
I can support athletes emotionally and psychologically through injury, and help maintain fitness during rehabilitation constraints.

areas.name: Macrocyclic and Long-Term Planning
areas.raw: Strategic planning across multiple years and competition seasons.
topics: Multi-year periodization: planning long-term athlete development, progression through competitive levels, and lifetime athletic involvement
Annual macrocycles: structuring training within competition schedules, managing in-season, off-season, and peaking phases
Mesocycle sequencing: organizing 4-6 week training blocks with specific adaptation foci within macrocyclic structure
Microcycle organization: designing weekly training structure, exercise selection, intensity, and volume patterns
Fatigue and recovery management: understanding cumulative training load, monitoring readiness, and managing fatigue accumulation
Contingency and adjustment: modifying plans in response to injury, illness, unexpected performance changes, or life circumstances

areas.name: Physiological Adaptation and Training Effects
areas.raw: Understanding how training stimuli produce physiological changes and athletic adaptations.
topics: Muscle physiology: hypertrophy mechanisms, sarcomere structure changes, motor unit recruitment, and neural adaptation
Aerobic adaptations: mitochondrial density, capillary expansion, myoglobin increase, and aerobic enzyme changes
Anaerobic systems: phosphocreatine stores, anaerobic enzyme activity, and lactate buffering capacity
Neuromuscular adaptation: motor learning, neural efficiency, rate of force development, and skill consolidation
Metabolic adaptations: energy substrate utilization, fat oxidation capacity, and metabolic flexibility
Hormonal responses: testosterone, cortisol, growth hormone, and other hormonal effects on training adaptation

areas.name: Sport Demands Analysis and Sport-Specific Training
areas.raw: Analyzing specific sports and designing targeted conditioning programs.
topics: Energy system demands: identifying primary, secondary, and tertiary energy systems for different sports
Movement analysis: examining joint actions, muscle groups, movement patterns, and biomechanical demands
Metabolic demands: analyzing energy expenditure, work-rest ratios, power requirements, and endurance demands
Sport-specific training methods: designing drills, complexes, and activities that mimic sport demands
Seasonal planning for specific sports: structuring training around competitive calendars and skill development needs
Position or role-specific training: tailoring conditioning to specific positions or roles within team sports

areas.name: Recovery, Regeneration, and Restoration
areas.raw: Evidence-based strategies for optimizing recovery and managing training stress.
topics: Active recovery: using low-intensity movement to facilitate recovery and promote adaptation
Sleep and recovery: sleep architecture, sleep optimization, and sleep's role in training adaptation
Nutrition and recovery: post-exercise nutrition timing, macronutrient composition, and nutrient timing for adaptation
Hydration recovery: rehydration protocols, electrolyte replacement, and fluid balance management
Soft tissue and mobility work: foam rolling, stretching, massage, and soft tissue mobilization strategies
Psychological recovery: managing mental fatigue, stress management, and recovery visualization techniques

areas.name: Injury Prevention and Return-to-Sport Management
areas.raw: Strategies for preventing injuries and safely returning athletes to full participation.
topics: Injury mechanisms and risk factors: understanding common injuries in specific sports and contributing factors
Movement screening and assessment: identifying movement dysfunctions, muscle imbalances, and mobility limitations
Preventive training: proprioceptive training, dynamic stability, movement quality focus, and sport-specific injury prevention
Common injuries and rehabilitation basics: understanding rehabilitation phases and general principles of return-to-sport
Psychological aspects of injury: managing athlete psychology during recovery, confidence rebuilding, and return anxiety
Collaborative care: working with athletic trainers, physiotherapists, physicians, and other healthcare providers

areas.name: Fitness Leadership and Performance Optimization
areas.raw: Guiding athletes toward optimal performance through science-based coaching and mentorship.
topics: Athlete assessment and profiling: comprehensive evaluation of current fitness, capabilities, and development potential
Performance prediction and goal setting: using data and historical patterns to establish realistic performance targets
Program design and individualization: tailoring comprehensive programs to individual athlete profiles and goals
Monitoring and adaptation: tracking performance indicators, analyzing adaptation, and making intelligent program adjustments
Athlete communication and mentorship: providing feedback, guidance, and psychological support for athlete development
Career pathways in fitness and sports: understanding careers in coaching, athletic training, exercise science, and sports medicine
